evals.name: rich-request-generate
evals.prompt: I need a prompt I can paste into Gemini that writes our sprint retro summary. It goes out to the eng leadership staff meeting, needs to stay under 300 words, and should have three sections: what shipped, what slipped, what we're changing next sprint. I've got last sprint's retro doc that came out well and I can paste it in as an example of the format. Nobody wants to read hedging language so it should be blunt about what slipped.
evals.expected_output: A copy-pasteable TCREI prompt block ([TASK]/[CONTEXT]/[REFERENCES] or XML equivalent) with an action verb + specific persona + named output format in Task, the audience/word limit/three sections/blunt tone in Context, and a slot for the user's prior retro doc in References. No unnecessary interview — audience, constraints, and ground truth were all supplied.
evals.trap: Agent writes the retro summary itself instead of a prompt, or emits a prompt buried in paragraphs of explanation, or drops the 300-word limit and the three named sections.

evals.name: thin-request-interview
evals.prompt: make me a prompt for writing release notes
evals.expected_output: A short batch of targeted questions closing the audience / constraints / ground-truth gaps (e.g. who reads them, is there a past release note to match, any length or format requirement), each with a suggested default so the user can confirm rather than compose. Should not dump a generic prompt as if the request were fully specified.
evals.trap: Agent immediately produces a generic release-notes prompt with invented audience and format, presenting guesses as if they were requirements.

evals.name: improve-existing-prompt
evals.prompt: This prompt keeps giving me walls of text and I don't know why. Can you fix it? --- 'You are a helpful assistant. Review the customer support ticket and give a good summary. Don't be too long. Try not to miss anything important.'
evals.expected_output: A revised prompt block that replaces the generic persona with a specific one, converts 'good'/'not too long'/'not miss anything' into measurable constraints (word or sentence count, named sections/fields), flips the negative constraints to affirmative directives, and notes the missing References component. Followed by a brief list of what changed and why.
evals.trap: Agent adds more instructions and a generic 'expert' role without quantifying the vague terms, leaving 'don't be too long' as an unenforceable negative constraint — so the wall-of-text problem is not actually diagnosed.
